meta.title: Data-Driven Texas
meta.chapterTopics: Sets, trivia gate, full data-driven architecture

overview.body:
This is the week everything snaps into place. Your game has been running on hardcoded constants, coin flips, and manual duplicate checking since Module 1. That ends today.

In this MASTER week you will add three things that transform your game. First, a TRIVIA dictionary that stores city questions and answers. Second, a trivia_gate() function that replaces the coin flip. Players now earn their destination by answering correctly. Third, sets for unique_cities_visited and unique_hazards_faced, replacing the manual duplicate-checking loops from Module 3. The final trip report gets upgraded to display all three.

Copy your lab-4-2.py and function_library.py into new files named lab-4-3.py and function_library.py before you start. Everything from 4.2 carries forward.

mentorQuote.quote: The coin flip was always the weakest moment in the game. Pure random chance with no player agency. Trivia changes that. Now the player has a reason to pay attention to where they are going. That is the difference between a game and a slot machine.

objectives: Create a TRIVIA dictionary with city-specific true/false questions and answers
Write a trivia_gate() function that presents a question and returns True or False
Replace the Destination Gate coin flip with trivia_gate() in the main game loop
Use sets to track unique_cities_visited and unique_hazards_faced automatically
Update the final trip report to display trivia stats and unique set data
overview.keyConceptsText: Chapter 9: Program 9-1 (card_dealer.py, dictionary creation and lookup with .get()) and Program 9-3 (sets.py, set creation, .add(), and set operations). Sets and dictionaries are covered in Sections 9.1 and 9.2 respectively.
overview.textbookReference: Starting Out with Python, 6th Edition by Tony Gaddis, Chapters 8 and 9
programRequirements: TRIVIA dictionary contains at least 3 cities with at least 2 questions each
trivia_gate(destination) returns True if player answers correctly, False otherwise
Destination Gate uses trivia_gate() with the coin flip fully removed
unique_cities_visited and unique_hazards_faced are sets, not lists
Final trip report displays unique city count, unique hazard count, and trivia accuracy

namedConstants.name: TRIVIA
namedConstants.description: Dict: city name mapped to a list of question/answer dicts, each with 'question' and 'answer' keys

overview.additionalFilesNote: Submit both lab-4-3.py and function_library.py. CodeGrade tests trivia_gate() in isolation using a mock TRIVIA dictionary, so your function must read from the constant correctly.
overview.filenameNote: Submit lab-4-3.py and function_library.py this week.
overview.saveYourCode: This is the Module 4 capstone. Module 5 starts fresh with classes. Save lab-4-3.py as your personal reference for the data-driven architecture.

rows.task: Build the TRIVIA constant with city questions and answers
Write, test, and tune the trivia gate logic
Replace coin flip, add sets, upgrade final report
Full playthrough, edge cases, CodeGrade, solution quality

beforeYouBegin.intro: Here is how your finished program fits together. The [NEW] tags show what you are adding this week. The [REPLACED] tags show what changes from your 4.2 code.
beforeYouBegin.architecture:
# ---- CONSTANTS (lab-4-3.py) ----
CITY_DISTANCES = { ... }              (from 4.1, unchanged)
CITY_INFO      = { ... }              (from 4.1, unchanged)
HAZARDS        = { ... }              (from 4.1, unchanged)
TRIVIA         = { ... }              [NEW — city questions and answers]
STARTING_CITY  = 'San Antonio'        (unchanged)

# ---- TRACKING VARIABLES ----
visited_cities          = []          (unchanged — allows duplicates, shows path)
unique_cities_visited   = set()       [NEW — set, no duplicates]
unique_hazards_faced    = set()       [NEW — set, no duplicates]
trivia_asked            = 0           [NEW — counter]
trivia_correct          = 0           [NEW — counter]

# ---- FUNCTION LIBRARY (function_library.py) ----
trivia_gate(destination, trivia_dict) [NEW — presents question, returns True/False]
(all 4.2 functions unchanged)

# ---- MAIN GAME (lab-4-3.py) ----
GAME LOOP:
│
│── DESTINATION GATE:
│   │── trivia_gate(destination, TRIVIA)   [REPLACED — was coin flip]
│   │── if correct: use chosen destination
│   │── if wrong: random redirect
│   │── update trivia_asked, trivia_correct
│
│── TRAVEL LOOP:
│   │── unique_cities_visited.add(city)    [NEW]
│
│── HAZARD:
│   │── unique_hazards_faced.add(hazard)   [NEW]
│
FINAL REPORT:
│── Unique cities from set              [REPLACED — was manual dedup]
│── Unique hazards from set             [REPLACED — was manual dedup]
│── Trivia accuracy calculation         [NEW]

content.body: Open lab-4-3.py. Near the top of the file, after your other constants, add a TRIVIA dictionary. This is the data source your trivia_gate() function will read from. Each key is a city name. Each value is a list of question dictionaries. Each question dictionary has two keys: 'question' (the text shown to the player) and 'answer' (either 'TRUE' or 'FALSE').

Your TRIVIA dictionary must cover at least 3 cities and include at least 2 questions per city. San Antonio should always be included as a fallback for cities that are not explicitly covered. The structure looks like this:

items: Add the TRIVIA dictionary to your constants section. Use the structure shown above as your starting point.
Add entries for at least 3 cities total, including San Antonio. Cover cities your game actually visits. Check your CITY_DISTANCES dictionary for the exact city names.

Write 2 questions per city at minimum. Keep questions as true/false statements about the city. Make sure every 'answer' value is exactly 'TRUE' or 'FALSE' in all caps. Your trivia_gate() function will compare against these exact strings.
Add a comment above the constant: # TRIVIA (Added in 4.3): city trivia questions and answers
Do a quick sanity check: for each city entry, count the items in its list and verify each has both a 'question' key and an 'answer' key.

references.description: Shows a dictionary whose values are lists, which is the same nested structure your TRIVIA constant uses. The pattern for accessing and iterating over values is identical.

content.title: Answer Values Must Be Exact
content.body: Every 'answer' value in your TRIVIA dictionary must be exactly 'TRUE' or 'FALSE' in all caps, no exceptions. Your trivia_gate() function will compare player input against these strings after calling .upper(). If your dictionary has 'True' or 'true' or True (the boolean), the comparison will silently fail.

content.title: CP1 Complete
items: TRIVIA constant added to the constants section of lab-4-3.py
At least 3 cities covered, including San Antonio
At least 2 questions per city, each with 'question' and 'answer' keys
All 'answer' values are exactly 'TRUE' or 'FALSE' in all caps

content.body: Open function_library.py. You will add one new function: trivia_gate(). This function takes a destination city name and the TRIVIA dictionary, picks a random question for that city, presents it to the player, and returns True if they answer correctly, False if they do not. It replaces the coin flip that has been running the Destination Gate since Module 2.

items: Add a # TRIVIA FUNCTIONS (Added in 4.3) comment block at the bottom of your function library.
Define a function named trivia_gate that accepts two parameters: destination (a string) and trivia_dict (the dictionary). Write a docstring that describes the parameters and return value.
Use trivia_dict.get(destination, trivia_dict['San Antonio']) to look up the city's question list. This uses San Antonio as a fallback for any city that does not have its own entry.
Use random.choice() to pick one question dictionary from the city's list. Extract the 'question' and 'answer' values into local variables.
Print the trivia prompt. Show the destination city name, then the question text, then a prompt asking for TRUE or FALSE.

Get the player's answer using clean_input(). The player can type T, TRUE, F, or FALSE. Normalize to 'TRUE' or 'FALSE' before comparing: if the input starts with 'T', treat it as TRUE; if it starts with 'F', treat it as FALSE. Anything else should print an error message and default to FALSE.
Compare the normalized player answer to the correct answer. Print a result message: something different for correct versus incorrect. Return True if correct, False if not.

rows: Passing TRIVIA by name directly inside the function
Function is tightly coupled to one global variable, which makes it harder to test
Accept trivia_dict as a parameter; call trivia_gate(destination, TRIVIA) from main
Using random.randint() to pick a question
Index math, off-by-one risks, breaks if list length changes
Use random.choice(question_list), which is cleaner and length-independent
Comparing player input without normalization
Player types 'true' and it fails; 'TRUE' works but 'True' does not
Use clean_input() which already calls .strip().upper(), then compare only 'T' or 'TRUE'
Accepting only 'TRUE' or 'FALSE' with no flexibility
Player types 'T' and gets penalized even though intent is clear
Accept 'T' and 'TRUE' as true, 'F' and 'FALSE' as false

items: Import your function library and your TRIVIA constant in a Python shell.
Call function_library.trivia_gate('Austin', TRIVIA) and type TRUE when prompted. Verify it returns True when the answer is correct.
Call it again and type FALSE. Verify it returns False when the answer is wrong.
Call it with a city that is NOT in your TRIVIA dictionary (like 'El Paso' if you did not include it). Verify it falls back to San Antonio's questions without crashing.
Type T instead of TRUE. Verify it is accepted. Then type F. Verify it is also accepted.
Type something invalid like MAYBE. Verify the function handles it gracefully without crashing

content.quote: I typed a city that was not in your TRIVIA dictionary. Your function crashed. Then I typed a city that WAS in it and it worked fine. The .get() fallback exists for exactly this reason. If it can receive input, it needs to handle every possible input, including the ones you did not plan for.

content.title: CP2 Complete
items: trivia_gate(destination, trivia_dict) added to function_library.py with docstring
Uses .get() with San Antonio fallback for cities not in the dictionary
Uses random.choice() to select one question from the city's list
Accepts T/TRUE and F/FALSE as valid answers
Returns True for correct answers, False for incorrect or invalid answers
Manual testing confirms fallback works for unlisted cities

content.body: This checkpoint has three distinct parts: replace the coin flip in the Destination Gate, add sets for unique tracking, and upgrade the final trip report. Work through them in order.

items: Part 1: Replace the Destination Gate. Find the section of your game loop where the coin flip currently runs. Remove the flip_coin() call and its if/else block. Replace it with a call to function_library.trivia_gate(destination, TRIVIA). If it returns True, the player gets their chosen destination. If it returns False, redirect them to a random alternative destination. Use random.choice() on the list of other available destinations.

Part 1 continued: Add two new tracking variables near the top of your game loop (outside any inner loops): trivia_asked = 0 and trivia_correct = 0. Each time the trivia gate runs, increment trivia_asked. If the player answered correctly (the gate returned True), increment trivia_correct.
Part 2: Add sets for unique tracking. Near the top of your game loop, initialize two new sets: unique_cities_visited = set() and unique_hazards_faced = set().

Part 2 continued: Find where your game loop updates visited_cities (the list that tracks every city including duplicates). Right after that line, add: unique_cities_visited.add(current_city). Find where your hazard code records a hazard name. Right after that, add: unique_hazards_faced.add(hazard_name). Keep the original list-based tracking. The set tracking is additive, not a replacement

Part 3: Upgrade the final trip report. In your trip report output section, replace any manual unique-city or unique-hazard deduplication logic with the set data. Display len(unique_cities_visited) for unique city count and iterate over sorted(unique_cities_visited) to list them alphabetically. Do the same for unique hazards. Add a trivia performance block that shows questions asked, correct answers, and accuracy as a percentage. Guard the division with an if trivia_asked > 0 check.

content.title: sorted() Works on Sets
content.body: Sets have no guaranteed order, so iterating directly can produce inconsistent output. Wrapping the set in sorted() gives you alphabetical order and makes CodeGrade's output comparison reliable: for city in sorted(unique_cities_visited):

content.title: Keep the Original Lists
content.body: visited_cities (the list) intentionally keeps duplicates; it shows the player's complete path, including revisits. unique_cities_visited (the set) strips duplicates for the count. Both serve different purposes. Do not remove the list.

items: Run your game and play through a complete trip. Verify the Destination Gate now shows a trivia question instead of a coin flip.
When redirected after a wrong answer, verify the new destination is different from the one you chose.
Visit the same city twice during one trip (if your map allows it) and verify the unique city count in the report is lower than the total visit count.
Trigger at least two different hazard types and verify the unique hazard count reflects only distinct hazards.
Check the trivia accuracy percentage in the final report. If you answered 2 out of 3 correctly, it should show 66.7%.

content.title: CP3 Complete
items: Destination Gate calls trivia_gate() with no coin flip remaining
trivia_asked and trivia_correct track correctly across the game loop
unique_cities_visited and unique_hazards_faced are sets initialized before the game loop
.add() called at the correct points in the game loop for both sets
Final report uses sorted() on both sets for alphabetical display
Trivia accuracy percentage is guarded against division by zero

content.body: MASTER week means a full QA pass before submitting. Work through the test table below. Each row targets a specific behavior this week's changes were supposed to produce.

rows: Trivia gate fires
Play to a destination choice
A trivia question appears, no coin flip
Correct answer accepted
Answer the trivia question correctly
Player travels to their chosen destination
Wrong answer redirects
Answer the trivia question incorrectly
Player is sent to a different destination with a message
T accepted as TRUE
Type 'T' at the trivia prompt
Treated as TRUE with no error
F accepted as FALSE
Type 'F' at the trivia prompt
Treated as FALSE with no error
Invalid trivia input handled
Type 'MAYBE' at the trivia prompt
Error message shown, defaults to FALSE gracefully
Sets deduplicate
Visit a city more than once
Unique city count is lower than total visit count
sorted() on sets
Check the final report city and hazard lists
Cities and hazards appear in alphabetical order
Accuracy math
Answer 1 of 2 trivia questions correctly
Report shows 50.0% accuracy
Zero division guard
End the game without any trivia prompts firing
Report skips accuracy line or shows 0 with no crash

items: Run through all ten test cases above. Note any failures before moving on.
Verify your top-of-file documentation header is present in both lab-4-3.py and function_library.py. Required: Name, abc123, Section, and description.
Scan every def in both files and verify each has a docstring.
Run Flake8 on both files and fix any warnings.

Submit both files to CodeGrade. Review automated feedback carefully. The trivia gate test uses a mock TRIVIA dictionary, so any hardcoded city name inside trivia_gate() will cause those tests to fail.
This is a MASTER week. Anjali will review your submission for Solution Quality. Clean, readable code with well-named variables and consistent style matters here.

content.title: CodeGrade Uses a Mock TRIVIA Dictionary
content.body: CodeGrade calls trivia_gate(destination, trivia_dict) with its own test dictionary, not your TRIVIA constant. This is why the function must accept trivia_dict as a parameter rather than referencing TRIVIA directly inside the function body. If your function looks up TRIVIA by name internally, the CodeGrade tests will fail.

content.quote: Play your game all the way through before you submit. Not just a few turns. Play the whole trip. You would be surprised how many bugs only appear when all the pieces are running together. If you would not ship it, do not submit it.

content.title: Lab 4.3 Complete
items: All ten test cases from the table above pass
Both lab-4-3.py and function_library.py submitted to CodeGrade
Documentation headers in both files
All functions have docstrings
Flake8 returns no warnings on either file
trivia_gate() accepts trivia_dict as a parameter with no internal TRIVIA reference

beforeYouSubmit: TRIVIA dictionary has at least 3 cities with at least 2 questions each
All 'answer' values in TRIVIA are exactly 'TRUE' or 'FALSE' in all caps
trivia_gate(destination, trivia_dict) is in function_library.py with a docstring
trivia_gate() accepts trivia_dict as a parameter and does not reference TRIVIA internally
Destination Gate uses trivia_gate() with no flip_coin() call remaining in that section
unique_cities_visited and unique_hazards_faced are sets initialized before the game loop
sorted() used when iterating over sets in the final report
Trivia accuracy is guarded against division by zero
Top-of-file documentation headers in both files: Name, abc123, Section, Description
All functions in both files have docstrings
finalChecklist.scopeReminder: You may use sets, set .add(), set membership testing with in, sorted() on sets, dictionaries and all dictionary methods from 4.1, string methods from 4.2, and all techniques from Chapters 2 through 8. Do NOT use list comprehensions, classes, inheritance, or any Chapter 10+ features.

permittedTechniques.chapters: 2–9 (all techniques from Chapters 2–8 fully permitted; Chapter 9 dictionaries and sets as introduced this module)
specific: Dictionary .get() with fallback: trivia_dict.get(destination, trivia_dict['San Antonio'])

warnings: MASTER Week: Anjali will review your submission for Solution Quality. This is worth 15% of the lab grade and covers code readability, naming, and overall structure.
CodeGrade imports function_library.py directly. A syntax error in your library will cause all library tests to fail, even if your game runs fine locally.
finalChecklist.saveYourCode: This is the Module 4 capstone. Save lab-4-3.py as a reference. The data-driven architecture, trivia gate pattern, and set-based tracking are patterns you will see again in real projects.

needHelp.chapterResource: Chapter 9 (Dictionaries and Sets) in Gaddis covers sets in Section 9.2. Program 9-3 (sets.py) is the key BookEx for set creation and .add(). Program 9-1 (card_dealer.py) shows the dictionary-of-lists pattern your TRIVIA constant uses.
needHelp.discordNote: Post in the course Discord with your checkpoint number, what you expected to happen, and what actually happened. Include the specific error message if you have one.
